verdict: All three posts and newsletter meet the depth and specificity standard. The friction vs. persuasion framing is precise and non-generic, the reactance and IKEA effect applications are VRM-specific, and the newsletter genuinely extends the posts with actionable re-engagement scripts for each of the five barriers.
strengths: Post 1 correctly frames the stall as a friction problem before prescribing the diagnostic question. The script lands in a credible register.
Post 2 effectively combines two psychological concepts (reactance and IKEA effect) without making either feel academic. The self-managing owner scenario is specific.
Post 3 introduces the distance concept clearly and closes with a concrete small-step offer structure that operators can use in their next conversation.
Newsletter delivers genuine extension of the posts: covers all five Berger barriers with a specific re-engagement script for each. The cost-of-inaction section adds material not in any of the posts.
Owner type references (Controller in Post 2) are correct and specific, not generic.
Scripts throughout read as real conversation language, not sales training manual language.
problems: Post 3 Oratia mention is slightly brief and could integrate more smoothly into the surrounding field note structure.
